name: Market Hall, Chipping Campden
subtitle: The Journey Begins
description: The iconic 17th-century Market Hall marks the official northern start of the Cotswold Way. From here, 102 miles of stunning Cotswold landscape stretch south to Bath.

name: Dover's Hill
subtitle: The Olimpick Viewpoint
description: This dramatic natural amphitheatre has hosted the Cotswold Olimpick Games since 1612. The viewpoint offers sweeping views across the Vale of Evesham to the Malvern Hills and beyond.

name: Fish Hill
subtitle: The Cotswold Rooftop
description: Fish Hill is one of the highest points on the northern Cotswolds, where the A44 crosses the escarpment. The picnic area offers stunning views westward over the Vale of Evesham.

name: Broadway Tower
subtitle: The Highest Castle in the Cotswolds
description: At 312m above sea level, Broadway Tower offers panoramic views across 16 counties. This 18th-century folly is one of the most photographed landmarks on the trail.

emergencyInfo.nearestPhone: Patchy signal — best near car park

name: Broadway Village Green
subtitle: The Jewel of the Cotswolds
description: Broadway's wide main street lined with honey-coloured limestone buildings has attracted artists and writers for centuries. A perfect resupply stop.

name: Snowshill Lavender View
subtitle: Ridge Above the Golden Village
description: From this ridge above Snowshill, the trail offers panoramic views across rolling Cotswold countryside. The village below is famed for its lavender fields and eccentric Snowshill Manor.

name: Stanway House
subtitle: A Hidden Cotswold Gem
description: Stanway House is a stunning Jacobean manor with the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world. The trail passes through its peaceful grounds.

name: Hailes Abbey
subtitle: Ruins of Medieval Pilgrimage
description: Founded in 1246, Hailes Abbey was a major pilgrimage site housing a phial of the Holy Blood. The atmospheric ruins managed by English Heritage sit in a peaceful meadow setting.

name: Sudeley Castle Gate
subtitle: A Royal Cotswold Castle
description: Sudeley Castle was home to Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII, who is buried in the chapel. The castle and its award-winning gardens have welcomed visitors for centuries.

name: Winchcombe Town Centre
subtitle: Ancient Capital of Mercia
description: Once the capital of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Mercia, Winchcombe is a charming market town with excellent pubs, shops, and the famous Sudeley Castle nearby.

name: Belas Knap
subtitle: 5,000 Years of History
description: One of the best-preserved Neolithic long barrows in England, dating to around 3000 BC. The false entrance and four burial chambers have yielded the remains of 38 individuals.

name: Postlip Hall
subtitle: Gateway to Cleeve Common
description: This historic manor marks the start of the climb onto Cleeve Common, the highest point of the Cotswold Way. The tithe barn and chapel date from the 13th century.

name: Cleeve Common
subtitle: Iron Age Camp & Cheltenham Views
description: The Cotswold Way crosses the open expanse of Cleeve Common, the largest area of unimproved limestone grassland in the Cotswolds. Near the path lie the earthwork ramparts of an Iron Age hillfort — one of the highest in England at over 300 metres. The common is rich in wildflowers including bee orchids, kidney vetch, and harebells, with skylarks singing overhead in summer. From the escarpment edge, panoramic views stretch across Cheltenham to the Malvern Hills, Brecon Beacons, and on clear days, the Black Mountains of Wales.

emergencyInfo.nearestPhone: Variable mobile signal on the common

name: Dowdeswell Reservoir
subtitle: Peaceful Waters Below the Escarpment
description: This scenic reservoir provides a tranquil rest point where the trail drops from the high wolds. Birdwatchers frequent the area for its resident and migratory species.

name: Seven Springs
subtitle: Source of the Thames?
description: Seven Springs claims to be the true source of the River Thames, as the source of the Churn, its longest tributary. A plaque marks the spot where water emerges year-round.

name: Leckhampton Hill
subtitle: The Devil's Chimney
description: Leckhampton Hill is famous for the Devil's Chimney, a natural limestone pillar left standing after centuries of quarrying. The escarpment views over Cheltenham are spectacular.

name: Crickley Hill
subtitle: Ancient Hillfort Above Cheltenham
description: This Iron Age hillfort and country park sits on the Cotswold escarpment with commanding views over Cheltenham and the Severn Vale. Archaeological finds date back 6,000 years.

name: Barrow Wake
subtitle: Guardian of the Ermin Way
description: This Bronze Age burial mound offers one of the finest viewpoints on the trail, looking out over Gloucester, the Forest of Dean, and the Black Mountains of Wales.

name: Birdlip Hill
subtitle: The Hilltop Village
description: This hilltop village has served travellers since Roman times, when the road from Cirencester climbed the escarpment here. The Royal George Hotel has refreshed walkers for centuries.

name: Cooper's Hill
subtitle: The Cheese Rolling Hill
description: Famous worldwide for its annual cheese rolling race, where competitors chase a 9lb Double Gloucester down the near-vertical slope. The Cotswold Way crosses this iconic hillside.

name: Cranham Corner
subtitle: Deep in the Beechwoods
description: A woodland crossroads where ancient beech trees create a cathedral-like canopy. Nearby Prinknash Abbey has produced pottery and incense since Benedictine monks settled here in 1928.

name: Painswick Beacon
subtitle: Hilltop Fort with a View
description: Also known as Kimsbury Camp, this Iron Age hillfort crowns the escarpment above Painswick. On clear days, the panorama extends to the Brecon Beacons across the Severn Vale.

name: Painswick, St Mary's Churchyard
subtitle: Queen of the Cotswolds
description: Known as the 'Queen of the Cotswolds', Painswick's churchyard is famous for its 99 clipped yew trees. Legend says the devil always kills the 100th.

name: Edge Village
subtitle: The Quiet Hamlet
description: This peaceful hamlet sits on the escarpment edge with the Edgemoor Inn offering refreshment to weary walkers. The name says it all — Edge sits right on the Cotswold edge.

name: Standish Church
subtitle: A Medieval Rest Stop
description: The Grade I listed Church of St Nicholas has served the Standish community since Norman times. Its peaceful churchyard offers walkers a sheltered rest on the trail.

name: Scottsquar Hill
subtitle: Woodland Ridge Between the Vales
description: The Cotswold Way passes through the wooded ridge of Scottsquar Hill, where ancient woodland cloaks the escarpment between the Severn Vale and the Stroud valleys. The trail weaves through stands of beech and oak, with glimpses of the hills beyond. In spring, the woodland floor is carpeted with bluebells and wood anemones. The area around Standish Park has been wooded since at least the medieval period, and the mixed deciduous woodland supports a rich variety of birds including nuthatches, treecreepers, and great spotted woodpeckers.

name: Standish Wood
subtitle: Into the Ancient Forest
description: Standish Wood is an ancient beech woodland carpeted with bluebells in spring. The trail winds through dappled light with occasional views over the Severn Vale.

emergencyInfo.nearestPhone: Very limited signal in woods — move to open ground

name: Coaley Peak Viewpoint
subtitle: The Severn Valley Panorama
description: Coaley Peak picnic site offers one of the finest views on the entire trail — a sweeping panorama across the Severn Vale to the Forest of Dean and the Welsh hills beyond.

name: Uley Bury
subtitle: Iron Age Fort Above the Valley
description: One of the largest Iron Age hillforts in England, enclosing 32 acres on a dramatic promontory. The deep ramparts and ditches are still clearly visible after 2,500 years.

name: Cam Long Down
subtitle: The Whale-Back Ridge
description: This distinctive whale-back ridge extends for nearly a mile along the escarpment, offering uninterrupted views across the Severn Vale to the Forest of Dean and Welsh hills.

name: Dursley Town Centre
subtitle: Gateway to Stinchcombe Hill
description: Dursley is a market town at the base of the escarpment. Resupply here before the push south through Stinchcombe Hill and beyond to Wotton-under-Edge.

name: Stinchcombe Hill
subtitle: The Drakestone Viewpoint
description: A circular walk around Stinchcombe Hill takes in Drakestone Point, one of the finest viewpoints on the southern Cotswold Way, looking across to the Mendip Hills and beyond.

name: Tyndale Monument, North Nibley
subtitle: Tower of the Bible Translator
description: This 34-metre tower commemorates William Tyndale, who first translated the New Testament into English. Climb the 121 steps for panoramic views across the Severn Vale.

name: Wotton Hill
subtitle: Above the Edge Town
description: Rising above Wotton-under-Edge, this hill offers views stretching to the River Severn. The Jubilee Plantation on the hilltop was planted to mark Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee.

name: Wotton-under-Edge
subtitle: Little Town on the Edge
description: Wotton-under-Edge sits at the foot of the escarpment with a heritage of wool trading. The town offers good food, accommodation, and the last major resupply before Old Sodbury.

name: Hawkesbury Upton
subtitle: Village on the Edge
description: This hilltop village offers essential supplies and the nearby Somerset Monument commemorates General Lord Somerset who fought at Waterloo. A natural rest point on the southern trail.

name: Horton Court
subtitle: Norman Hall in the Hills
description: This National Trust property contains one of the oldest domestic buildings in England — a 12th-century Norman hall. The Cotswold Way passes close to this hidden gem.

name: Little Sodbury Hillfort
subtitle: Where Tyndale Found His Voice
description: This Iron Age fort is where William Tyndale served as chaplain to Sir John Walsh, first conceiving his plan to translate the Bible. The earthworks command views across south Gloucestershire.

name: Old Sodbury
subtitle: Iron Age Fort and Ancient Paths
description: Old Sodbury sits on a ridge with the remains of an Iron Age hillfort. From here, the trail descends toward the final stretch into Bath.

name: Dodington Park Gate
subtitle: Through Capability Brown's Parkland
description: The Cotswold Way passes through the parkland of Dodington House, designed by Capability Brown. The grand Palladian mansion is glimpsed through the ancient trees.

name: Tormarton
subtitle: The A46 Crossing
description: This quiet village marks the crossing of the ancient Fosse Way (now A46). The Compass Inn has refreshed travellers since the coaching era, making it a natural trail rest point.

name: Pennsylvania
subtitle: Ridgeway Hamlet
description: This tiny hamlet on the ridge between Dyrham and Cold Ashton takes its name from settlers who emigrated to William Penn's colony. The garage shop offers last supplies before Bath.

name: Dyrham Park
subtitle: Ancient Deer Park & Battlefield
description: The Cotswold Way passes through the grounds of Dyrham Park, a magnificent National Trust property set in 270 acres of ancient parkland. The baroque mansion was built for William Blathwayt, Secretary of State to William III, in the late 17th century. The surrounding deer park, home to a herd of fallow deer, has roots stretching back to Saxon times. In 577 AD, this landscape witnessed the Battle of Dyrham — a pivotal clash where the Saxons defeated the Britons, capturing the cities of Bath, Cirencester, and Gloucester and reshaping the map of early England.

emergencyInfo.nearestPhone: Variable mobile signal — try higher ground

name: Cold Ashton Village
subtitle: Stone Village on the Wold
description: The Cotswold Way passes through the churchyard of Holy Trinity, with its fine Perpendicular tower. The manor house nearby was built by a prosperous 16th-century wool merchant.

name: Cold Ashton
subtitle: The Final Ridge
description: Cold Ashton is a tiny village perched on the final ridge before Bath. The Elizabethan manor and medieval church are worth a pause before the triumphant descent.

name: Grenville Monument, Lansdown
subtitle: Civil War Battlefield
description: This monument marks where Sir Bevil Grenville fell at the Battle of Lansdown in 1643 during the English Civil War. The Royalist commander's death turned a tactical victory into strategic defeat.

name: Prospect Stile, Lansdown
subtitle: First Glimpse of Bath
description: This famous viewpoint behind Bath Racecourse gives walkers their first full panorama of Bath spread out below — the Royal Crescent, the Abbey, and the golden city in its valley.

name: Kelston Round Hill
subtitle: The Final Summit
description: The last hilltop before the descent into Bath. From this rounded summit, the entire trail unfolds in memory as walkers prepare for the final two miles into the city.

name: Weston Village
subtitle: The Last Village Before Bath
description: The trail passes through All Saints churchyard in this charming village on Bath's western edge. From here, it's just one mile along the river into Bath city centre and the Abbey.

name: Bath Abbey
subtitle: Journey's End
description: Bath Abbey marks the southern terminus of the Cotswold Way. After 102 miles, you've earned a celebration — and perhaps a visit to the famous Thermae Bath Spa.
